channel.title: healthTest
channel.description: This is a randomly ordered feed of the most recent entries from healthTest, skipping any feeds with errors.

item.title: Using saliva to detect disease holds promise, but it's not perfected yet
item.description: Easier than a blood test, saliva tests have the potential to detect cavities, infections and even cancer. But a lack of insurance coverage and other o...

item.title: Penpulimab and lenalidomide in combination with standard of care chemoimmunotherapy demonstrates promising safety and efficacy in diffuse large B-cell lymphoma
item.description: 1. Penpulimab and lenalidomide in combination with rituximab, gemcitabine, and oxaliplatin (R-GemOx regimen) is safe, tolerable, and shows antitumour ...

item.title: Artificial intelligence predicts colorectal cancer in ulcerative colitis with 99% accuracy
item.description: 1. A new AI workflow utilizing large language models can predict with 99% accuracy which ulcerative colitis patients will remain free of colorectal ca...

item.title: For years the Taliban told women to cover up in public. Now they're cracking down
item.description: At hospitals, at seminaries and on buses, the Taliban is stepping up enforcement of rules on women's dress in the city of Herat.

item.title: Facing a mental health crisis, an NJ school pulled a beloved novel from English class
item.description: Junot DÃ­az's The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao was removed from an English class at the public school. PEN America says it's part of a trend of scr...

item.title: Physician burnout: Finding peace in a broken health care system

item.title: Uneasy at the dentist? You're not alone
item.description: Settling into a cushy reclining chair and closing your eyes while soft music plays is appealing, except perhaps at the dentist's office. According to ...

item.title: Healthy Life Expectancy Declines Again
item.description: Years spent in good health have dropped to their lowest level since comparable data were first collected in 2011 to 2013, with stark regional gaps acr...

item.title: Reviewing locum tenens agreements: Look beyond the hourly rate
item.description: I was halfway through reviewing what looked like an excellent locums opportunity when I realized the hourly rate was the least important number in the...

item.title: Key alterations discovered in the cerebral cortex of people with psychosis
item.description: Researchers at the University of Seville have analyzed alterations in the cerebral cortex in people suffering from psychosis. Their findings show that...

item.title: Why âeat less, move moreâ fails for midlife weight loss
item.description: Clinicians often notice a frustrating pattern: Women in midlife follow standard weight-loss advice, eat less and move more, yet see little progress. C...

item.title: Lipedema Is Now Recognized as a Chronic Disease
item.description: International consensus defines lipedema as a chronic disease; clarifies its biology and diagnosis; and urges standardized care, research, and policy ...

item.title: When a Breast Cancer Surgeon Gets Breast Cancer
item.description: Anne Peled, MD, helped pioneer a sensation-preserving mastectomy procedure. When she got the diagnosis, she needed the procedure herself. Hereâs what ...

item.title: Stunning new maps of myelin-making mouse brain cells advance understanding of nervous system disorders
item.description: Johns Hopkins scientists say they have used 3D imaging, special microscopes and artificial intelligence (AI) programs to construct new maps of mouse b...

item.title: The misuse of hormone therapy in menopause care
item.description: As an integrative and functional medicine physician, I prescribe hormone therapy. I also see its misuse daily. Hormone replacement therapy (HRT), incl...

item.title: 'It's chronic disease, stupid!' The central challenge facing health care
item.description: "It's the economy, stupid!" is an aphorism coined by James Carvill during Bill Clinton's 1992 U.S. presidential campaign to keep workers focused on a ...

item.title: Certain Occupations Linked to Higher IBD Risk
item.description: According to a large international study, specific occupations were linked to higher IBD risk, with diesel exposure, work patterns, and socioeconomic ...

item.title: Birthing Techniques Linked to ADHD Risk
item.description: Using a vacuum followed by forceps was associated with a higher risk for ADHD than delivery by second-stage cesarean. Medscape Medical News

item.title: Why it's funnier when you're not allowed to laugh
item.description: I don't think I've ever laughed harder than during a church service, when something faintly ridiculous caught my eye. My friend saw it too, and once s...

item.title: Artificial intelligence designed drugs Hit 90% Phase I success rate in trials
item.description: 1. AI-native biotechnology firms are reporting Phase I success rates between 80% and 90%, nearly doubling the historical industry average of approxima...

item.title: 'Dude, good luck': The Olympic wit and wisdom of a pioneering African skeleton racer
item.description: Who says serious athletes are always serious? Akwasi Frimpong, who's competed for Ghana, is a world-class wisecracker as he reflects on being a Black ...

item.title: That ain't perfume! Ancient bottle contained feces, likely used for medicine
item.description: Researchers found a tiny bottle from ancient Rome that contained fecal residue and traces of aromatics, offering evidence that poop was used medicinal...

item.title: Sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 inhibitors may improve left ventricular diastolic function in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
item.description: 1. Sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 inhibitors (SGLT-2i) were associated with improved left ventricular diastolic function and New York Heart Associatio...
